next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 18

By the time the early rains stopped arriving as an event and settled into habit, the camp had learned a different set of noises. In the dry weeks the stilts used to complain in thin sharp ticks, heat pulling pitch from the boards and shrinking every nail hole around them. Now the wood gave longer sounds. Water moved inside it. The main house let out slow damp groans before dawn, and the screened walkways snapped when wind shoved rain sideways through them, as if the whole place were flexing sore joints in its sleep.

Mina woke before the generator came on because something dripped against metal in the room next to hers with the patience of a clock. Her room had been the linen closet once. Imogen had taken out half the shelves, left the rest, and called it a practical solution in the voice she used when practicality was covering for poverty. Mina slept under spare mosquito netting, on a narrow camp mattress that smelled faintly of bleach and old salt, with a coil of replacement screen spline hanging from a hook by the door and a stack of folded warning placards under the bed. On the wall above her duffel, clipped to a thumbtack, the latest receipt from the Gulf Wildlife Board curled at one corner where the air never dried. Payment received. Balance still ugly.

Rain had blown in through the outer screen overnight. The toes of her work shoes were wet. She pulled them on anyway and stepped into the hall in shorts and a camp shirt so worn the old logo had faded to a pink smear across her chest. The main house smelled of coffee left sitting on a burner too long, we...
open_hooks_to_carry: Tomas takes them out one last time without engines or extraction gear.
They witness luminous spawning traces or eggs in the roots, enough to know the line has become part of the creek.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...no one was supposed to go now.

"Yes," she said. "Probably."

Then she looked back at Mina, and the plainness in her face was the opposite of abandonment.

"That does not make it wrong."

She touched Mina's forearm with two fingers, exactly once, in full view of nobody who mattered anymore, then took the recorder case and went down the steps toward the bat lab, toward contaminated files and grant trouble and a life that had not chosen ease either.

Mina stayed where she was after everyone else had found a direction. Heat climbed. Her job was gone. By evening her name would move through email chains and board texts and whatever shallow legal language people used when they wanted human mess to harden into categories. Some version of disgraced again would attach itself to her before the sun went down. That part was already done.

Below the platform, the blackwater shifted under mangrove shade, mirror-dark until a breeze wrinkled it. No blue showed. No miracle announced itself on cue. The only thing the confession had changed immediately was the vocabulary on land. The eel was not a windfall now. Not a savior. Not a donor deck with a mascot. It had become evidence, contamination, protected risk, agency review. Ugly names, all of them. She could live with ugly names if they bought the creature one more week of not being turned into a logo or a specimen tray.

By the time the launch engines coughed back to life and carried the officials away from the platform, no one had called the eel a discovery in hours. That, for now, was the nearest thing to protection Mina knew how to give.
state_deltas_required: Impending enforcement, a damaged rookery, and the last chance to decide whether knowledge will mean capture or care
Mina, Rhea, and Tomas choose protection over ownership by witnessing the eel without taking a specimen or giving precise coordinates.
